questions.prompt: Values 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 are inserted in order into a plain (non-self-balancing) BST. What happens, and why does it matter for real systems?

options.text: Every node gets only a right child, degrading the tree into a linked list with O(n) height — a realistic case, since sorted or near-sorted input (timestamps, auto-increment IDs) is common in production data, not a rare adversarial edge case.
The BST automatically rebalances itself during insertion to maintain O(log n) height.
This causes a runtime error because BSTs cannot accept sorted input.
The tree remains balanced because insertion order does not affect BST shape.

questions.explanation: Plain BSTs have no self-correction mechanism; sorted insertion is the textbook worst case, producing O(n) height and degrading every operation to O(n) — and unlike a contrived interview example, sorted-ish real data is genuinely common, which is precisely why production key-value structures use self-balancing variants. (b) plain BSTs (as distinct from AVL/red-black) have no rebalancing logic at all. (c) BSTs accept any input including sorted; there's no error, just a bad shape. (d) insertion order is exactly what determines BST shape when no rebalancing exists.

questions.prompt: Java's TreeMap and C++'s std::map both use red-black trees rather than AVL trees, despite AVL trees offering tighter balance (faster lookups in theory). Why?

options.text: Red-black trees require fewer rotations on insert/delete on average, favoring general-purpose write performance, while AVL's tighter balance mainly benefits read-heavy workloads — a general-purpose standard library defaults toward the more balanced (write vs. read) trade-off.
AVL trees cannot guarantee O(log n) search in the worst case, while red-black trees can.
Red-black trees use less memory per node than AVL trees in all cases.
This is purely a historical accident with no remaining technical justification.

questions.explanation: Both structures guarantee O(log n) worst-case height — the difference is the constant-factor trade-off between insert/delete cost (rotations) and lookup speed, and standard libraries optimize for the general-purpose mixed workload rather than a read-only extreme. (b) is false; AVL also guarantees O(log n) worst-case search, that's the whole point of AVL's balance invariant. (c) memory-per-node is comparable between the two (a color bit vs. a height/balance field) — not the deciding factor. (d) there's a real, current, cited technical rationale (write-cost trade-off), not mere historical inertia.

questions.prompt: You need to serialize a binary tree to a string such that it can be deserialized back into the exact same shape. Which traversal is required, and why?

options.text: Preorder — visiting a node before its children means a parser reading the serialized output left-to-right encounters each parent before its subtrees, enabling top-down reconstruction.
Inorder — because it produces sorted output, which is easiest to parse.
Postorder — because children should always be created before their parent in any reconstruction algorithm.
Any traversal order works equally well for serialization, since all four visit every node exactly once.

questions.explanation: Preorder's parent-before-children order is exactly what a recursive-descent deserializer needs: read a value, know it's the current subtree's root, then recursively build left and right from what follows. (b) inorder alone cannot reconstruct tree *shape* — it only recovers a BST's sorted values, not arbitrary tree structure, and for a general (non-BST) tree it carries no shape information at all. (c) postorder can also support reconstruction with a different (bottom-up) parsing strategy, but it's not the 'required' answer preorder is, and it's less directly aligned with top-down parsing. (d) traversal order materially changes what information is recoverable — they are not interchangeable for this purpose.

questions.prompt: You need to compute, for every node, the size of its subtree (count of descendants including itself), where each node's answer depends on its children's answers. Which traversal fits?

options.text: Postorder — children are fully resolved before the parent computes its own value, which is exactly the dependency direction this problem requires.
Preorder — because the parent's value should be computed first.
Inorder — because sorted order is required for any counting problem.
Level-order — because breadth-first processing is always required for aggregation problems.

questions.explanation: Any 'a node's answer depends on its children's answers' problem is a postorder-shaped problem by definition — resolve the dependencies (children) before the dependent (parent). This is the same pattern as the chapter's balanced-height check. (b) preorder computes the parent first, before the children's sizes are even known — backwards for this dependency. (c) inorder's sorted-order property is irrelevant to a counting problem with no ordering requirement. (d) level-order processes by depth, not by dependency order, and doesn't naturally support 'children before parent' at all.

questions.prompt: A teammate's 'is this tree balanced' check calls a separate height(node) function (which recomputes height from scratch via full recursion) at every node during a traversal. What's the complexity flaw, and the fix?

options.text: height() is recomputed from scratch for every node visited by isBalanced(), making total work O(n^2); the fix is a single postorder pass that computes height and detects imbalance together, returning early (e.g. -1 sentinel) once an imbalance is found.
The recursion in height() is infinite and will never terminate.
isBalanced() should use BFS instead of recursion to correctly detect imbalance.
There is no performance issue; both functions are already O(n).

questions.explanation: Each call to isBalanced() at a node triggers a full height() recursion on both subtrees — and this happens again at every level of the tree, multiplying the redundant work; for a tree of height h, a node near the root triggers height() calls that redo work already done lower down, yielding O(n^2) total in the worst case (e.g., a balanced tree of depth log n still incurs this at every one of its n nodes). The chapter's fix computes height and balance in one bottom-up pass, propagating a sentinel value (-1) upward the instant an imbalance is found, so each node is visited once — O(n) total. (b) the recursion is well-founded (base case at null) and terminates normally. (c) BFS doesn't inherently fix the redundant recomputation; the fix is about avoiding repeated work, not traversal order. (d) is the misconception this question exists to correct.

questions.prompt: Why does an inorder traversal of a valid BST always produce values in sorted order?

options.text: The BST invariant (left subtree all smaller, right subtree all larger) guarantees that visiting left-subtree, then node, then right-subtree at every level processes every value in strictly increasing order by construction.
Inorder traversal internally sorts the values before visiting them.
It only works if the tree happens to already be balanced.
It's a coincidence specific to small trees and doesn't hold for large ones.

questions.explanation: This is a direct, guaranteed consequence of the BST invariant itself, applied recursively at every node — it holds regardless of the tree's shape or balance, precisely because the invariant is enforced at every single node, not just the root. (b) inorder traversal does no sorting; it simply visits nodes in an order that a valid BST's invariant already guarantees is sorted. (c) balance affects height/performance, not this correctness property — even a completely degenerate (linked-list-shaped) BST still yields sorted inorder output. (d) the property holds for BSTs of any size, by the same recursive argument.
